narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 played the biggest role in the model's decision, as it stood out well below the typical 3.24 average among students who went on to pass the bar exam on their first try, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting a failure. Next, your LSAT score of 31 had a notable negative influence too, falling short of the 37.27 average seen in successful bar takers, further tipping the scales against you. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile ranking of 5 was actually a bit stronger than the average for passers at around 5.91, which helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success and softened the overall assessment. Coming from a lower-middle income family, like only about 9% of those who passed, and planning full-time studies didn't sway things much in this case, though full-time enrollment is common among successful students. Overall, the model estimated a 60% chance you'll fail the bar exam on the first attempt, which is why your law school application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. This prediction focuses purely on academic predictors like grades and test scores, without considering gender, race, or other personal background. Compared to typical accepted students, your profile showed weaker entry credentials that historically correlate with bar struggles, even with some solid later-year performance. It's tough to hear, especially when you've pushed through to a decent Year 3 decile despite earlier challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and explore ways to strengthen your academic record, perhaps through post-bac courses or a master's program to lift that GPA perception. Many students in similar situations improve their profiles and get accepted elsewhere—reach out to admissions counselors for targeted advice on appealing or reapplying. You've already shown resilience in climbing deciles, so building on that could shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pursuing your law goals; persistence often pays off.
